items.passage: Prices in a market economy emerge from the constant interaction between buyers and sellers. When demand for a product rises but supply stays fixed, the price tends to climb until some buyers withdraw. Producers respond to higher prices by manufacturing more goods, which gradually pushes the price back down. This self-correcting mechanism guides resources toward the products that people value most. Economists describe the balance point as the equilibrium, where the quantity supplied matches the quantity demanded.

items.passage: Languages are never static systems; they shift steadily across generations of speakers. New words enter a vocabulary as technologies appear, while older terms quietly fall out of everyday use. Pronunciation also drifts, so that sounds once distinct may gradually merge into a single form. Contact between communities accelerates these changes, since speakers borrow expressions from neighboring groups. Linguists track such patterns to reconstruct how related languages diverged from a common ancestor long ago.

items.passage: Sound travels as a wave of pressure that ripples outward through air, water, or solid materials. The source vibrates and pushes nearby molecules together, although the molecules themselves barely move from their resting spots. These pulses reach the ear, where delicate structures convert the motion into signals the brain interprets. The pitch of a sound depends on how rapidly the source shakes each second. Loudness, by contrast, reflects the amount of energy carried by the advancing wave.

items.passage: An alloy is a metal created by combining a base element with one or more additional substances. Adding modest amounts of carbon to iron, although seemingly minor, produces steel that is far stronger than either ingredient alone. The extra atoms disturb the orderly arrangement of the metal, making it harder for layers to slide past each other. Engineers adjust these mixtures carefully to obtain qualities such as resistance to rust or extreme heat. Because of this flexibility, alloys appear in nearly every modern machine and structure.

items.passage: Ocean currents are vast rivers of water that circulate continuously around the globe. Surface currents are driven mainly by steady winds that drag the upper layers of the sea along. Deeper movements, however, arise from differences in temperature and salinity that alter the density of the water. Cold, salty water tends to sink toward the seafloor and slowly spreads across entire ocean basins. Together these flows redistribute heat from the equator toward the poles, shaping climates on distant coastlines.

items.passage: Clouds form when warm, moist air rises and cools to the point where water vapor can no longer remain invisible. As the air ascends, its temperature drops until the vapor condenses onto tiny floating particles of dust or salt. Millions of these droplets often gather together to produce the visible masses overhead. The shape of a cloud, however, reveals much about the conditions in the atmosphere at that altitude. When the droplets grow heavy enough, they fall toward the ground as rain or snow.
